info.name: Bitcoin – Tx Status Lookup (GET /tx/{txid}/status · GET /tx/status)
info.description:
E2E tests for the live bitcoin transaction-status lookup endpoints.

Routes under test:
  GET /api/v1/bitcoin/tx/{txid}/status   — single-transaction status lookup (JWTAuth required)
  GET /api/v1/bitcoin/tx/status          — batch status lookup via ?ids= (JWTAuth required)

The durable CRUD resource is covered separately in e2e/bitcoin/tx-crud.json.

Folder execution order:
  1. setup                    — register, verify email (via Gmail), login → txstatus_access_token
  2. unauthenticated          — requests without JWT → 401
  3. validation-single        — single-tx path-param guards (invalid txid format)
  4. happy-path-single        — GET /tx/{txid}/status with known txids → correct status + shape
  5. validation-batch         — batch query-param guards (missing ids, too many, invalid txid)
  6. happy-path-batch         — GET /tx/status?ids=... with multiple txids → correct map shape
  7. rate-limiting            — IP limiter exhaustion: 20 warmup → ok, 21st → 429

Pre-requisites:
  - BTC_NETWORK=testnet4 in .env.
  - TRUSTED_PROXIES=127.0.0.1/32 in .env so X-Forwarded-For rewrites work.
  - Run `make _e2e-kv-clean` before each run to reset Redis state.
  - Run `make _e2e-db-clean` between runs to remove the setup user.
  - gmail_access_token must be a valid Gmail OAuth2 token set in the Postman environment.
  - txstatus_known_txid must be set in environment.json to a testnet4 txid known to the node.
  - txstatus_mempool_txid (optional) — a txid currently in the node's mempool.

Notes on what is NOT covered here (covered by internal unit tests):
  - isHex validation correctness                    — handler_test.go
  - Uppercase txid normalisation                    — handler_test.go
  - ErrRPCUnavailable → 502                         — handler_test.go (mock TxQuerier)
  - GetMempoolEntry fallback logic                  — service_test.go
  - Conflicting / abandoned status paths            — service_test.go
  - Duplicate txid deduplication in batch           — handler_test.go
  - Metric recording (OnTxStatusResolved)           — service_test.go

exec: // Increment the request sequence counter and derive a unique X-Forwarded-For IP
// for every request so non-RL folders never exhaust any rate-limit bucket.
// Rate-limiting folder overrides _xff in its own prerequest script.

item.name: setup
item.description: Registers the gmail_test_email user, fetches the OTP from Gmail, verifies the email, then logs in to obtain txstatus_access_token. All downstream folders depend on this token being set.

exec: console.error('[ERROR] gmail_test_email not set in environment — add it to environment.json.');
if (!token) { console.warn('[WARN] gmail_access_token not set — skipping Gmail fetch'); return; }
console.log('[INFO] Waiting 8 s for verification email to arrive in Gmail...');
if (err) { console.error('[ERROR] Gmail list failed:', err); return; }
if (res.code === 401) { console.error('[ERROR] Gmail token expired'); return; }
if (!msgs || !msgs.length) { console.warn('[WARN] No matching email found in Gmail.'); return; }
if (err2) { console.error('[ERROR] Gmail fetch failed:', err2); return; }
const p = parts.find(x => x.mimeType === 'text/plain') || parts.find(x => x.mimeType === 'text/html');
if (!body) { console.warn('[WARN] Could not decode email body.'); return; }
if (!match) { console.warn('[WARN] No 6-digit OTP found in email body.'); return; }
console.log('[INFO] OTP stored in txstatus_code:', match[1]);
console.error('[ERROR] txstatus_code is empty — Gmail fetch did not complete.');
console.error('        Check: gmail_access_token is set and not expired, --delay-request >= 8000.');

item.name: unauthenticated
item.description:
Requests without a valid JWT must be rejected before handler logic runs.
The IP rate-limiter (btc:txstatus:ip:) runs first (unique XFF from collection prerequest), then deps.JWTAuth rejects.

item.name: validation-single
item.description:
Path-param validation for GET /tx/{txid}/status. All requests are authenticated.
The handler normalises txids to lowercase and rejects any value that is not a 64-character hex string.

item.name: GET /tx/{txid}/status — txid too long (65 chars) → 400 invalid_txid
GET /tx/{txid}/status — txid contains non-hex chars → 400 invalid_txid
happy-path-single
item.description:
Successful GET /tx/{txid}/status lookups. Uses txstatus_access_token from setup.

txstatus_known_txid must be set in environment.json to a txid the node knows about.
The test asserts the response shape but does not assert the exact status value, since
the node's view of any given txid can change over time (confirmations increase, etc.).

A not_found lookup is performed against the all-zeros txid, which no real transaction
can ever have.

item.name: GET /tx/{txid}/status — known txid → 200 with valid status shape

exec: console.warn('[WARN] txstatus_known_txid not set or invalid in environment.json — this test may fail.');
console.log('[INFO] Bitcoin node not reachable (expected in offline CI).');
pm.test('status field is a valid TxStatus', () => {
pm.test('confirmed: confirmations is a positive integer', () => {
pm.test('confirmed: block_height is a positive integer', () => {
// The all-zeros txid can never exist on any real chain.
// The handler reaches the service, which returns not_found (no RPC error).
// If the Bitcoin node is unreachable the handler returns 502 instead;
// we accept either outcome and skip the not_found assertion in that case.
console.log('[INFO] Bitcoin node not reachable (expected in offline CI).');

item.name: GET /tx/{txid}/status — uppercase txid is normalised → 200 (same result as lowercase)

exec: // Build the uppercase variant of the known txid.
// The handler normalises txids to lowercase before processing.
// Submitting an UPPERCASE txid must produce the same HTTP 200 result
// (or 502 if the node is down) — never a 400 invalid_txid.
pm.test('response body has a status field', () => {

item.name: validation-batch
item.description:
Query-param validation for GET /tx/status?ids=... All requests are authenticated.
Guards checked in handler order: missing ids → 400, >20 ids → 400, any invalid txid → 400.

exec: // Build a comma-separated list of 21 valid txids.

item.name: GET /tx/status — one invalid txid in batch → 400 invalid_txid

exec: // One valid txid followed by one invalid one — handler rejects the whole batch.

item.name: GET /tx/status — txid in batch is 63 chars (too short) → 400 invalid_txid
happy-path-batch
item.description:
Successful GET /tx/status?ids=... lookups. Uses txstatus_access_token from setup.

The response is a JSON object with a 'statuses' map keyed by txid.
Each value must have the same shape as a single-tx status response.
Duplicate txids in the request must collapse to a single map entry.

item.name: GET /tx/status — single txid in batch → 200 statuses map with 1 entry

exec: console.log('[INFO] Bitcoin node not reachable (expected in offline CI).');
pm.test('statuses key is the queried txid', () => pm.expect(body.statuses).to.have.property(txid));
pm.test('entry has a valid status field', () => {

item.name: GET /tx/status — two distinct txids → 200 statuses map with 2 entries

exec: console.log('[INFO] Bitcoin node not reachable (expected in offline CI).');

item.name: GET /tx/status — duplicate txids collapse to 1 entry

exec: // Sending the same txid twice must produce exactly ONE map entry.
// Deduplication is performed in the handler before the service call.
console.log('[INFO] Bitcoin node not reachable (expected in offline CI).');

item.name: GET /tx/status — maximum allowed (20 txids) → 200 statuses map

exec: // Build 20 distinct-looking txids by varying the last byte.
// They will all resolve as not_found but the handler must accept all 20.
console.log('[INFO] Bitcoin node not reachable (expected in offline CI).');

item.name: GET /tx/status — uppercase txids in batch are normalised → 200

exec: // Uppercase txids must be accepted and normalised to lowercase.
// The response map key must be the lowercase form.
console.log('[INFO] Bitcoin node not reachable (expected in offline CI).');

item.name: rate-limiting
item.description:
Verifies the btc:txstatus:ip: IP rate limiter (burst=20, rate=20/60s).
Expected Retry-After: ceil(1 / (20/60)) = ceil(3) = 3 seconds.

All requests use a fixed XFF (127.0.0.6) to isolate this bucket from other bitcoin folders.
Requests without a JWT still consume rate-limit tokens — the limiter runs before JWTAuth,
so warmup requests return 401 but count toward the bucket.

We use 21 warmup requests (burst=20 + the initial token already consumed) then assert
the 22nd is rejected. The burst is 20 per the Routes() configuration.

variable.description: Auto-incremented counter for unique X-Forwarded-For IPs.
Current X-Forwarded-For value. Set by collection prerequest; overridden to 127.0.0.6 in rate-limiting folder.
Set by setup folder from gmail_test_email.
OTP fetched from Gmail by setup/register test script.
JWT access_token from a successful login; used by all non-setup folders.
Uppercase variant of txstatus_known_txid; set by happy-path-single/uppercase test prerequest.
Comma-separated list of 21 txids; populated by validation-batch/too_many prerequest.
Comma-separated list of 20 distinct txids; populated by happy-path-batch/max prerequest.
